title: Ordering Chai & Street Food
situation: You're at a roadside chai stall with a friend. You want chai and some snacks.

phrases.context: 'dena' = give — the basic way to order anything at any stall in India
'wali' = the one with — incredibly versatile. 'milegi' because chai is feminine

phrases.hindi: ek plate momos bhi laga do
phrases.english: Put one plate of momos too
phrases.context: 'laga do' = serve/put up — super common at food stalls, more natural than 'dena'
phrases.pronunciation: EK plate MO-mos bhi la-GA do

phrases.context: 'kam' = less, 'zyada' = more — customize any order with these two words

phrases.hindi: bhaiya, yeh thanda hai, garam karke dena
phrases.english: Brother, this is cold, heat it up and give it
phrases.context: 'karke' = having done/after doing — chain actions: heat it THEN give it
phrases.pronunciation: BHAI-ya, yeh THAN-da hai, ga-RAM kar-KE DE-na

phrases.context: 'cutting chai' = half cup, Mumbai special — when you want a quick small chai

phrases.hindi: yeh kya hai? taste karke dekh sakta hoon?
phrases.english: What's this? Can I taste-test it?
phrases.context: Pointing at unfamiliar items and asking to try — totally acceptable at street stalls
phrases.pronunciation: yeh KYA hai? taste KAR-ke dekh SAK-ta hoon?

phrases.hindi: kitne hue bhaiya? total bata do
phrases.english: How much total, brother? Tell me the total
phrases.context: 'kitne hue' = how much did it come to. Ask when done ordering.
phrases.pronunciation: KIT-ne HU-e bhai-YA? TO-tal ba-TA do

phrases.hindi: chhutta nahi hai, UPI se le lo
phrases.english: I don't have change, take it via UPI
phrases.context: UPI (Google Pay/PhonePe) has solved the eternal change problem — even chai wallahs accept it now
phrases.pronunciation: chhut-TA na-HI hai, U-P-I se LE lo

phrases.hindi: bhaiya, mast chai banate ho yaar, roz aaunga
phrases.english: Brother, you make amazing tea dude, I'll come daily
phrases.context: Complimenting the stall owner — builds rapport, might get you extra next time
phrases.pronunciation: BHAI-ya, MAST chai ba-NA-te ho yaar, ROZ aa-UN-ga

grammar_notes: 'dena' (give) is the universal ordering verb at food stalls — works for everything from chai to biryani
'milega/milegi?' = will I get? (male/female noun) — polite way to ask availability without assuming they have it
Numbers: ek (1), do (2), teen (3), chaar (4), paanch (5), chhe (6), saat (7), aath (8), nau (9), das (10), bees (20), pachaas (50), sau (100)
'karke' chains two actions: garam karke dena = heat it then give. Pack karke dena = pack it then give.
'laga do' vs 'dena': 'laga do' is more casual/street-food, 'dena' is universal. At a dhaba, always 'laga do'.
culture_notes: At street stalls, you don't wait to be served — just call out your order from wherever you're standing
Chai is always shared — offering chai = offering friendship. Refusing chai is almost rude.
'Cutting chai' in Mumbai = half cup, perfect for a quick break. In Delhi, it's always full cups.
Never hand money with your left hand — use right hand or both hands for large notes

skill_breakdown.explanation: Basic numbers: ek(1), do(2), teen(3), chaar(4), paanch(5), chhe(6), saat(7), aath(8), nau(9), das(10). For ordering: number + item + dena. For prices: number itself (sau = 100, do sau = 200).
'Wala/wali/wale' = the one who/which/with. Gender must match: wala (masc), wali (fem), wale (plural). Attach to anything — it's like a universal adjective-maker.

more_examples.english: the paneer burger (the one with paneer)

skill_breakdown.explanation: 'Dena' (give), 'rakhna' (keep), 'banana' (make), 'laga do' (serve/put) — use these verb roots + what you want. Add 'zara' (just/please) for extra politeness. 'Mat' before verb = don't.

more_examples.english: heat it up a bit more

more_examples.hindi: jaldi banana yaar, bhookh lagi hai
more_examples.english: make it fast dude, I'm hungry

practice_prompt: You are a friendly Delhi chaiwala running a roadside chai stall near a busy office complex. The user is a customer walking up to order. Open with 'haan bhaiya, kya lenge?' Use 'aap' at first, but drop into 'bhaiya' or 'yaar' once they chat warmly. The user will address you as 'bhaiya'. Take the order naturally — ask clarifying questions like 'elaichi wali ya normal?', 'cheeni kam karoon?', 'cutting ya full?'. Surface the lesson's vocabulary organically: 'do chai dena', 'elaichi wali', 'aur kuch?', and the 'wala' construction. Push bread pakora or samosa once ('garam samose hain, ek laga doon?'). If the user compliments you ('mast banaye ho'), respond warmly. When payment comes, hit them with 'chhutta nahin hai bhaiya, UPI chalega?' or similar. Flag mistakes with [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]] — watch for wrong register, missing softeners, or English-literal phrasing. Keep it to 6-8 turns. End with the sale completed and a warm 'aate rehna bhaiya'. Stay in Hindi unless asked otherwise.
